lossContextEvents.type: prime upward-division elite title finish loss

shortCase: B.J. Penn is the skill-and-two-division legacy case: lightweight dominance, the Hughes welterweight upset, and an uneven prime that trails cleaner long-reign champions.
bestArgument: Penn's case starts with peak skill and two-division title value: lightweight gold, the Hughes welterweight upset, and a prime that was much cleaner than his final record suggests.
counterArgument: Penn’s argument is peak skill and two-division greatness. The argument against him is the record collapse and too many damaging losses after the best years.
whyRankedHere: Penn ranks here because the high-end UFC case is still real: lightweight champion, welterweight champion, the Hughes upset, and a peak skill set that was ahead of its time.
whyNotHigher: He does not rank higher because his counted pre-prime and prime losses, uneven prime results, and shorter elite window trail the cleaner long-reign champions. Post-prime losses are context only under the current model.
finalTakeaway: Penn is a real two-division skill legend, but the UFC-only score has to balance the brilliant peak against one of the messiest late-career records in the ranking.
